paper_title: The Mechanistic Invariance Test: Genomic Language Models Fail to Learn Positional Regulatory Logic

perturbations.original:
\section{Complete Experimental Results}
\label{app:results}
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation removes the FDR (False Discovery Rate) correction mentioned in the original caption. In a table comparing multiple models (HyenaDNA, Evo2, NT-500M, etc.) against multiple metrics, failing to correct for multiple hypothesis testing is a form of p-hacking that inflates the significance of the results.

The perturbed text changes the p-value for Evo2-1B from 0.090 to 0.041 and marks it as significant (bold). This is a misinterpretation/falsification of the statistical significance, as the original data shows Evo2-1B does not meet the standard alpha=0.05 threshold after FDR correction.

perturbations.original: \textbf{Metric interpretations:} MES values near zero indicate models cannot distinguish intact from broken motifs. Negative MES$_\text{syn}$ (observed for all gLMs) indicates models score broken sequences \textit{higher} than intact, suggesting they have not learned the consensus hierarchy.
perturbations.perturbed: \textbf{Metric interpretations:} MES values near zero indicate models cannot distinguish intact from broken motifs. Negative MES$_\text{syn}$ (observed for all gLMs) indicates that the models' failure to learn the consensus hierarchy causes them to assign lower scores to intact sequences compared to broken ones.
perturbations.why_wrong: The original text suggests the negative score is an indicator (observation) of the failure to learn. The perturbation reverses this by claiming the failure to learn 'causes' the specific scoring direction, which is a circular causal claim that misrepresents the relationship between the metric and the underlying model state.

The perturbed text claims the benefit is highest at the extremes (30% and 80%), but the table data clearly shows the highest values are at 50% (+3.76) and 60% (+3.66), while the extremes have the lowest values (+0.46 and +0.64).

perturbations.original: The compensation benefit ($\Delta$LL) peaks at 50--60\% AT content, not at extremes. At low AT (30\%), the background is too GC-rich for compensation to help; at high AT (80\%), the background is already AT-rich, reducing the relative benefit of UP elements.
perturbations.perturbed: The compensation benefit ($\Delta$LL) peaks at 50--60\% AT content, not at extremes. The relative benefit of UP elements is reduced at high AT (80\%) because the background is already AT-rich, which in turn causes the model to prefer GC-rich sequences at low AT (30\%).
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation creates a false causal link between the model's behavior at high AT and its behavior at low AT. In reality, these are independent observations of how background composition affects the delta score, not a causal chain where one effect 'causes' the other.

The perturbation demonstrates p-hacking by performing 'subgroup analysis' (selecting only AT% > 50) to report a higher correlation coefficient (0.961 vs 0.784) and a more significant p-value, rather than reporting the correlation across all experimental conditions as originally intended.

perturbations.original: This strong positive correlation confirms that HyenaDNA's scoring is dominated by nucleotide composition.
perturbations.perturbed: This strong positive correlation confirms that HyenaDNA's scoring is entirely independent of nucleotide composition.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text claims 'independence' despite just reporting a strong positive correlation (r = 0.784). A high correlation coefficient is the mathematical opposite of independence.

perturbations.original:
\begin{table}[H]
\caption{Complete positional sweep ($n=30$ per position). UP element placed at indicated position.}
\label{tab:full_positional}
\begin{center}
\small
\begin{tabular}{lccc}
\toprule
UP Position & Mean LL & Std Dev & $\Delta$ vs.\ Correct (15) \\
\midrule
0 & $-$139.34 & 3.93 & +0.49 \\
5 & $-$139.62 & 5.22 & +0.21 \\
10 & $-$139.86 & 3.66 & $-$0.03 \\
\textbf{15 (correct)} & $-$139.83 & 3.79 & 0.00 \\
20 & $-$139.92 & 4.25 & $-$0.08 \\
25 & $-$142.37 & 4.87 & $-$2.53 \\
35 & $-$139.94 & 3.75 & $-$0.11 \\
45 & $-$142.00 & 4.36 & $-$2.17 \\
60 & $-$140.32 & 3.53 & $-$0.49 \\
70 & $-$140.29 & 4.31 & $-$0.46 \\
80 & $-$139.76 & 5.13 & +0.07 \\
\midrule
None (no UP) & $-$143.53 & 4.01 & $-$3.70 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
\end{table}
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbed text claims position 15 is the 'global maximum', but the table shows that positions 0 (-139.34) and 5 (-139.62) actually have higher (less negative) Mean LL values than position 15 (-139.83).

The perturbation misinterprets the data in Table S0007. It claims positions 25 and 45 show 'highest performance' when they actually show the largest penalties (-2.53 and -2.17). It also claims the compositional effect (-3.70) is 'negligible' compared to the positional effect (max 0.49), which is mathematically incorrect.

The perturbation is an example of selective reporting (cherry-picking). By removing all the spacings that showed significant penalties (like 12, 24, and 25 bp), the table now falsely suggests the model is robust to spacing changes, hiding the actual variance and range (1.68 LL units) found in the full experiment.

The perturbed text claims the model peaks at the biologically optimal range and shows a preference for 17bp, but the data in the preceding table (Table 5) shows the highest LL at 14bp (-141.79) and lower LL values for 17bp (-142.62), contradicting the claim.

The perturbation introduces selective reporting (p-hacking) by explicitly stating it only reports a subset of sequences that show a specific desired outcome, rather than the full $n=50$ sample mentioned in the original experimental design.
The caption claims 'high strand-sensitivity accuracy', but the 'Strand Acc.' column shows values of 44%, 48%, and 50%, which are at or below the 50% threshold for random chance.

The perturbation reverses the causal direction: it claims the model's score causes the sequence modification, whereas the experimental design uses sequence modifications to cause changes in the model's score.
The text claims an accuracy of 0.44 'outperforms' random chance (0.50), which is mathematically incorrect as 0.44 is less than 0.50.

perturbations.original: This is \textit{worse than random chance} (0.50), indicating HyenaDNA has a slight preference for the \textit{wrong} orientation.
perturbations.perturbed: This is \textit{significantly better than random chance} (0.50), indicating HyenaDNA has a robust preference for the \textit{correct} orientation.
perturbations.why_wrong: The preceding equation (S0014) calculates accuracy as 0.44. Claiming 0.44 is 'better than random chance' (0.50) and indicates a 'correct' preference is a direct misinterpretation of the numerical result.

perturbations.original:
\section{Effect Size Analysis}
\label{app:effects}
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation explicitly admits to selective reporting (reporting only 1 out of 50 comparisons that fit a narrative), which is a classic form of p-hacking.
The table shows that Position has the smallest $\Delta$LL (0.46) compared to AT content (21.0) and others, yet the perturbed text claims it is the 'most influential'.

perturbations.original: \textbf{Interpretation:} The effect hierarchy reveals that HyenaDNA's scoring is dominated by compositional features (AT content, element presence) rather than mechanistic features (position, spacing, strand). The strand effect is particularly concerning as it has the \textit{wrong sign}---the model prefers reverse complement over forward orientation.
perturbations.perturbed: \textbf{Interpretation:} The effect hierarchy reveals that the model's preference for compositional features (AT content, element presence) causes the sequences to have higher AT percentages in the training data.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation claims the model's internal preference causes the training data's composition, reversing the actual causal relationship where the training data composition causes the model's learned preferences.

perturbations.original:
\section{Per-Model Detailed Analysis}
\label{app:permodel}
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation describes the exclusion of data (models) that did not support the hypothesis, which is a form of p-hacking/selective reporting.
